Name: neorv32_twi.ctrl.enable
Description: Global module enable bit that gates the TWI unit operation, drives engine.state(2), controls clock-generator reset behavior and the clkgen_en_o output.
Justification: Tampering with ctrl.enable (clearing or preventing it from being set) will stop the watchdog from starting/counting and thereby prevent the WDT from performing system recovery, causing a loss of the module's intended availability/safety function.

Name: neorv32_twi.ctrl.prsc
Description: Selects which bit of the external pre-scaled clock vector (clkgen_i) is sampled to produce the internal clk_gen.tick timing used by the phase generator and engine.
Justification: Tampering with the prescaler will alter uart_clk selection and thus change bit timing, which can corrupt transmissions and receptions. The primary risk is integrity degradation of serial data due to incorrect timing.

Name: neorv32_twi.ctrl.cdiv
Description: Fine-grained clock divider value used by the internal clock generator to determine when to assert clk_gen.tick (counts up to ctrl.cdiv).
Justification: ctrl.cdiv shapes tick generation and duty/hold timing; if altered, setup/hold windows and SCL high/low durations become incorrect, corrupting transfers even when logic otherwise behaves.

Name: neorv32_twi.engine.done
Description: Pulse asserted when a byte transmission/reception completes; used to write RX FIFO and to transition engine state back to IDLE.
Justification: engine.done delineates byte boundaries and triggers FIFO updates; spurious or missing pulses cause duplicated/dropped bytes and incorrect state transitions, corrupting the data stream.

Name: neorv32_twi.twi_sda_i
Description: External serial data input sampled and synchronized into io_con.sda_in_ff for bus sensing and as the sampled input during transmissions.
Justification: twi_sda_i is the truth source for received bits and ACK/NACK; corruption or mis-sampling yields wrong data and protocol markers, leading to silent data corruption or failed transactions.

Name: neorv32_twi.twi_sda_o
Description: Top-level SDA drive signal reflecting io_con.sda_out; actively drives SDA low to claim the bus and releases it to let the pull-up drive it high.
Justification: Incorrect SDA drive level or timing produces invalid start/stop edges or bit values and can break arbitration, corrupting transactions for all devices on the bus.

Name: neorv32_twi.engine.busy
Description: Indicates that a bus operation is currently in progress (derived from engine.state); used by status reads and IRQ generation.
Justification: If engine.busy is stuck high, software defers new operations indefinitely; if stuck low, overlapping requests can be issued that the engine cannot serialize, causing stalls or retries—both harm forward progress.

Name: neorv32_twi.twi_scl_i
Description: External SCL input sampled and synchronized to detect bus state and clock-stretching conditions via io_con.scl_in_ff.
Justification: twi_scl_i is required to detect clock stretching and phase completion; if not sensed correctly, the controller may wait forever or advance prematurely, wedging the bus and degrading availability.

Name: neorv32_twi.twi_scl_o
Description: Top-level SCL drive signal reflecting io_con.scl_out; used by the engine/clock generator to actively control the SCL line during phases.
Justification: If SCL cannot be driven/released at the right times, bit phases cannot progress and transfers stall or deadlock, directly impacting bus availability.

Name: neorv32_twi.clkgen_en_o
Description: Output reflecting ctrl.enable used to enable an external pre-scaler/clock source; exposes the module enable state to external clocking logic.
Justification: If clkgen_en_o is prevented from asserting or is driven incorrectly at integration, the prescaler/tick will not be provided and the watchdog counter will not progress, disabling the WDT functionality and preventing the module from performing its recovery/reset role (Denial-of-service on WDT).

Name: neorv32_twi.irq_o
Description: Signals the host that the TWI module is enabled and ready/idle (TX FIFO empty and engine idle); used by the host to schedule further actions.
Justification: If irq_o is suppressed, the host may not service the peripheral promptly, leading to idle time and stalled workflows; if asserted spuriously, it can trigger interrupt storms that starve normal processing—both harm overall availability.
